coredata.dc:title: Multi-objective optimization of lithium-ion battery designs considering the dilemma between energy density and rate capability
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Xiao-Ying
Zhang, Wen-Ke
Yin, Ying
Liu, Kailong
Yang, Xiao-Guang
coredata.dc:description: Electrified transportation requires batteries with high energy density and high-rate capability for both charging and discharging. Li-ion batteries (LiBs) face a dilemma: increasing areal capacity and reducing electrode porosity to boost energy density often reduces rate capability due to a longer and more tortuous ion transfer path. Tailoring cell design parameters to balance these metrics is essential but challenging. Here, we present a multi-objective optimization framework targeting energy density, fast charging, high-rate discharging, and lifespan simultaneously. Four cell parameters—cathode areal capacity, N-P ratio, cathode porosity, and anode porosity—along with operating temperature, are selected as design variables. A physics-based pseudo-2D model, validated against experimental data, generates data to train the surrogate model, which is combined with the NSGA-II algorithm for rapid optimization. Three different objective calculation methods are compared to identify the maximum sum of energy densities, lowest polarization, and most balanced performance, respectively. Cell design parameters are optimized at different temperatures using the most balanced optimization method. Results demonstrate that elevating cell operating temperature achieves high-rate capability while maintaining high energy density, mitigating the energy-power trade-off and broadening battery design parameter ranges.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Multi-objective optimization
Battery design
Fast charging
Lithium plating

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of three different objective calculation methods on optimization
Effects of temperature on multi-objective optimization
Temperature as an additional optimization variable by considering cell degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Li-ion cell metrics under different design parameters. Effects of cathode areal capacity and electrode porosity on the (a) 0.1C discharge energy density, (b) 3C discharge energy density, and (c) 10-minute fast-charging energy density. (d) The maximum values in each type of energy density for single-objective optimization (derived from maximum value in ①, ②, and ③).

Li-ion cell utilization rate under different design parameters. Utilization rate at 3C discharge with variation of (a) cathode porosity and (b) anode porosity and 10-min charge with variations of (c) cathode porosity and (d) anode porosity.

Temperature effects on cell high-rate metrics under different design parameters. Temperature effects on 3C discharge energy density with variations of (a) cathode areal capacity and (b) cathode porosity and 10-min charge energy density with variations of (c) cathode areal capacity and (d) anode porosity.

An efficient multi-objective optimization framework. Firstly, obtain the parameter space through the design of experiments. Subsequently, the battery performance data is obtained through the simulation of the P2D model and employed as the training set for the surrogate model. Further, a surrogate model is employed to establish a mapping of battery design parameters and operating temperature to performance. Finally, the surrogate model is combined with NSGA-|| algorithm to achieve fast optimization of battery performance.

Multi-objection optimization results comparison of 3 cases at 25 °C. (a) summary of 3 different calculations and final results (b) results comparison of the sum of 3 energy densities (c) electrolyte concentration distribution at the end of charging or discharging time (d) results comparison of derivations from the mean value.

Effects of elevated temperature on Li-ion cell performance and design parameters. Comparison of (a) single-objective optimization cell performance at 25 °C and multi-objective optimization cell performance at 60 °C (b) multi-objective optimization cell design parameters at different temperatures (25 °C, 45 °C, 60 °C).
Effects of cell design parameters (a) cathode areal capacity (b) NP ratio (c) cathode porosity (d) anode porosity on total driving range of electric vehicle under different temperature.

Results of single-objective optimization and multi-objective optimization. (a) optimized cell performance and (b) optimized key parameters in maximum total range single-objective optimization. (c) optimized cell performance and (d) optimized key parameters by considering maximum total range, 0.1C discharge energy density, 3C discharge energy density, 10-min charging energy density as multi-objectives.

head.ce:title: Multi-objective optimization of lithium-ion battery designs considering the dilemma between energy density and rate capability

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Control Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrified transportation requires batteries with high energy density and high-rate capability for both charging and discharging. Li-ion batteries (LiBs) face a dilemma: increasing areal capacity and reducing electrode porosity to boost energy density often reduces rate capability due to a longer and more tortuous ion transfer path. Tailoring cell design parameters to balance these metrics is essential but challenging. Here, we present a multi-objective optimization framework targeting energy density, fast charging, high-rate discharging, and lifespan simultaneously. Four cell parameters—cathode areal capacity, N-P ratio, cathode porosity, and anode porosity—along with operating temperature, are selected as design variables. A physics-based pseudo-2D model, validated against experimental data, generates data to train the surrogate model, which is combined with the NSGA-II algorithm for rapid optimization. Three different objective calculation methods are compared to identify the maximum sum of energy densities, lowest polarization, and most balanced performance, respectively. Cell design parameters are optimized at different temperatures using the most balanced optimization method. Results demonstrate that elevating cell operating temperature achieves high-rate capability while maintaining high energy density, mitigating the energy-power trade-off and broadening battery design parameter ranges.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The challenges of energy crisis and environmental pollution have accelerated the shift towards the electrification of transportation. With rapid technological advancements and cost reductions over the past decade, electric vehicle (EV) sales have surged, surpassing 14 million units annually by 2023[Anon., 1]. Despite the significant progress, the global penetration rate of EVs in 2023 is only 15.8 %. Particularly, the market penetration for medium- and heavy-duty vehicles is only 1.2 %. Other areas, such as electric vertical take-off and landing vehicles (eVTOLs), have garnered considerable attention in recent years but have faced slow progress due to battery technology challenges.

In general, EVs require batteries with high energy density, high power density, fast charging capability, long lifetime, excellent safety, and low cost [2]. Energy density determines driving range, power density affects vehicle acceleration, fast charging capability impacts refueling time, battery lifespan influences vehicle service life, and battery safety is crucial for passenger protection. However, Li-ion batteries (LiBs), the most widely used battery technology today, are well known for their trade-off nature ‒ improving one metric often results in the sacrifice of another. For example, increasing areal capacity and reducing electrode porosity can enhance energy density but significantly decrease power density and fast-charging capability due to a longer and more tortuous ion transfer path. Similarly, lowering electrolyte viscosity (e.g., by adding esters as co-solvents) can improve fast-charging and low-temperature performance but compromise thermal stability and safety. Therefore, optimizing battery design to balance these various demands is critical. For instance, in developing extreme fast charging (XFC) batteries, the U.S. Department of Energy has set a goal to achieve three metrics simultaneously: a charging time <10 min, a charged energy density >180 Wh/kg, and a cycle life exceeding 500 cycles [Anon., 3].

A critical barrier to LiBs is the dilemma between energy and power densities ‒ increasing energy density often reduces power density. As mentioned above, a typical way to improve energy density is to increase the areal mass loading of anode and cathode active materials while reducing electrode porosity [4]. However, as analyzed theoretically by Gallagher et al. [5], increased electrode thickness and reduced porosity result in higher ion transfer resistance in the porous electrodes, leading to electrolyte depletion at high C-rates and consequently poor rate capabilities for both charging and discharging. Particularly, electrolyte depletion in the anode during charging could trigger the deposition of metallic lithium on graphite surfaces, known as lithium plating. The plated lithium is highly reactive and can react with electrolyte to cause rapid capacity fade. For instance, Gallagher et al. [5] experimentally showed that a cell with 3.3 mAh/cm² areal capacity cannot withstand 1.5C charging, while a cell with 4.4 mAh/cm² areal capacity cannot even take 1C charging. Apart from the rapid capacity loss, lithium plating can potentially trigger internal shorting, leading to hazardous consequences [6–7]. Therefore, lithium plating is widely recognized as the most critical challenge to the fast charging of LiBs.

Given the above energy-density versus power-density dilemma, it is essential to carefully tailor cell design parameters according to the specific requirements of the batteries in different application scenarios. For instance, when energy density is the primary concern, densely packed materials with high areal mass loadings should be used [8–12]. Conversely, when fast charging or high-rate discharge is more desired, energy density should be sacrificed by reducing the areal capacities. Essentially, the trade-off between energy and power densities stems from the conflict between increasing the packing density of active materials (to reduce the fraction of inactive materials) and the resulting increased ion transfer resistance due to longer and more tortuous ion flow paths.

Hence, it can be inferred that there are three key parameters affecting the energy-power dilemma: areal capacity, porosity, and electrode thickness for both anode and cathode. For areal capacity, an important parameter in cell design is the negative-to-positive capacity ratio (hereafter referred to as NP ratio). The NP ratio should be greater than 1.0 to prevent lithium plating [13–16], but a higher NP ratio means more excess anode materials, reducing the energy density. For porosity [17–21], higher porosity means more volume of electrolyte, thereby lowering energy density, while reduced porosity leads to higher electrolyte transfer resistance and poorer rate capability. Electrode thickness is determined by the areal capacity and designed porosity (e.g., for a fixed areal capacity, increasing thickness increases porosity, and vice versa). Indeed, we should note that the three parameters are mutually restrictive: knowing two of them will determine the third when the electrode composition is fixed (i.e., fixed mass fraction of active materials, binders, and conductive agents).

A critical challenge in determining the above three parameters in battery design is their nonlinear effect on energy and power performance. For instance, the effective values of ionic conductivity and electrolyte diffusivity in the porous electrodes are typically calculated by multiplying the bulk values by a Bruggemann correction term: ψeff=ψbulkεn , where ε is the porosity, and n is a Bruggemann factor typically between 1.5 and 3.0, influenced by the electrode tortuosity. Thus, a slight decrease in porosity, though increasing energy density, may result in a substantial rise in ion transfer resistance, significantly deteriorating the cell's fast-charging capability. Similarly, when the areal capacity reaches a certain threshold, a further increase in areal capacity yields minimal gains in energy density but can cause a considerable drop in rate capability. Therefore, it is vitally important to devise a strategy or method that can optimize these parameters to achieve a delicate balance, ensuring the overall performance of the battery meets the specific application requirements.

Various efforts have been made in the literature to optimize design parameters for achieving optimal battery performance. De et al. [22] comprehensively investigated the effects of cathode and anode thickness and porosity on cell energy density, taking maximum energy density as the optimization objective, they ultimately obtained optimal design parameters under a specified current density. However, this study focused solely on a single objective (i.e., energy density) for optimization. Regarding multi-objective optimization, efforts have been made to address charging strategy optimization [23,24], pack structure optimization [25,26], cell structure optimization [27,28], and manufacturing process optimization [29,30]. Despite these advances, there is still no comprehensive optimization that simultaneously targets high energy density, lithium plating-free fast charging, high-rate discharge, and battery lifespan. Moreover, in today's battery product development, battery design parameters are still determined by trial-and-error method, which is time-consuming, costly, and often fail to achieve optimal results when based solely on the empirical methods. Additionally, operating temperature is also a key factor affecting battery performance. Most batteries are designed to work at a temperature range of ∼ 10 °C-45 °C, but several recent studies have revealed that elevating temperature could significantly enhance battery rate performance, such as achieving extreme fast charging.

To overcome the aforementioned limitations, this paper proposes a multi-objective optimization framework targeting energy density, fast charging, high-rate discharging, and lifespan simultaneously. Four cell parameters ‒ cathode areal capacity, NP ratio, cathode porosity, and anode porosity ‒ along with operating temperature, are selected as design variables. A physics-based pseudo-2D (P2D) model, validated against experimental data, is used to generate data to train the surrogate model, which is combined with the Non-Dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II)algorithm for rapid optimization. Three different objective calculation methods are compared to identify the maximum sum of energy densities, lowest polarization, and most balanced performance, respectively. Cell design parameters are optimized at different temperatures using the most balanced optimization method. The results demonstrate that elevating cell operating temperature could achieve a high-rate ability while maintaining a high energy density, thereby mitigating the energy-power trade-off and broadening the battery design parameter ranges.

In this work, we first utilize an electrochemical-thermal coupled model, also known as the P2D model, to explore the effects of cell design parameters on energy and power dilemma. The model is calibrated against experimental data from three different types of cells with varying design parameters (Fig. S1) to ensure accurate predictions of cell performance across a wide range of cell design parameters. As mentioned above, the energy-power dilemma is significantly influenced by the cell design parameters of areal capacity, porosity, and thickness of both the anode and cathode. Therefore, we select four design parameters (Fig. S2) - cathode areal capacity, NP ratio, cathode porosity, and anode porosity ‒ along with operating temperature as variables to calculate the main cell metrics.

Here, we use the discharge energy density at 0.1C rate, hereafter referred to as 0.1C energy density as the metric for evaluating cell energy density. The high-rate discharging performance is assessed by the discharge energy density at 3C rate, hereafter referred to as 3C discharge energy. Additionally, the fast-charging capability is evaluated as the maximum charged energy density in 10 min without lithium plating, hereafter referred to as 10-minute fast-charging energy. This 10-min fast-charging energy is assessed by adopting a multi-stage constant current charging protocol with 10 % state of charge (SOC) intervals (Fig.S3). At each SOC interval, a maximum charging current is estimated based on the criterion of maintaining the anode potential above 5 mV (to prevent lithium plating), and this current is applied throughout the corresponding 10 % SOC interval. The charging process stops once the total charging time reaches 10 min.

The P2D model is calibrated against experimental data from three different types of cells: an NMC532/Graphite cell and two NMC622/Graphite cells with different design parameters. The experimental data for the NMC532/Graphite cell and the 1-NMC622/Graphite cell are obtained from testing in our lab, and the experimental data for the 2-NMC622/Graphite are obtained from the author's previous publication [32]. The detailed experiment steps of the NMC532/Graphite cell and the 1-NMC622/Graphite cell are as follows:

During the charging rate capability test, the cell is initially charged in 0.1C/0.3C/1C/3C/5C Constant Current (CC) mode until it reaches the upper cut-off voltage (4.3 V), then in Constant Voltage (CV) mode until the current decreases to 0.05C, after 10 min rest, the cell is discharged in 0.2C CC mode until it achieves the lower cutoff voltage (2.7 V), then following 10 min rest. During the discharging rate capability test, the cell is charged in 0.2C CC mode until it reaches the upper cut-off voltage (4.3 V), then in CV mode until the current decreases to 0.05C, after 10 min rest, the cell is discharged in 0.1C/0.3C/1C/3C/5C CC mode until it achieves the lower cutoff voltage (2.7 V), then following 10 min rest. All experiments above are tested at 25 °C. During the 1C discharge test under different temperatures, the cell is charged at 25 °C in 0.2C CC mode until it reaches the upper cut-off voltage (4.3 V), then in CV mode until the current decreases to 0.05C, after 10 min rest, the cell is discharged at 32 °C/60 °C in 1C CC mode until it achieves the lower cutoff voltage (2.7 V), then following 10 min rest.

As mentioned above, four key cell design parameters ‒ cathode areal capacity, NP ratio, cathode porosity, and anode porosity ‒ are selected to evaluate the total energy density, 3C discharge energy, and 10-minute fast-charging energy at 25 °C, as shown in Fig. 1a–c. The 0.1C energy density is primarily related to the areal mass loading of the active materials. Consequently, an increase in the cathode areal capacity, in conjunction with a reduction in porosity, results in a notable enhancement of the 0.1C energy density. However, the 10-minute fast-charging energy and 3C discharging energy decrease dramatically at high areal capacities. Fig. 1d illustrates the instances when each of the three energy densities is at its maximum value, corresponding to the values of the remaining two energy densities. In the case of the single highest energy density, the other two energy density values are relatively low, presenting a challenge in achieving a balance between high energy density, fast charging, and high-rate discharging. Compared to the cathode areal capacity and porosity, the NP ratio exerts a relatively minor influence on the fast-charging and high-rate discharging performance (Fig. S4).

In order to further analyze the influence of cathode and anode porosity on the high-rate charging and discharging performances, Fig. 2 presents the variation in cell utilization rate with changes in porosity. For reference, the original data can be found in Fig. S5. Cell utilization rate is defined as the ratio of the 10-minute fast-charging energy density and 3C discharging energy density to the 0.1C discharging energy density. This ratio indicates the extent of fast charging or discharging energy density approaches the theoretical value. Furthermore, the ratio provides insight into the extent of cell polarization, as the polarization is minimal at the low rate of 0.1C.

In the case of 3C discharge, on the one hand, the results of cell utilization rate of low areal capacity with different cathode and anode porosities are consistent, indicating that the polarization is already very small at the large discharge rate, and that the effect of changing the porosity on it is not significant. On the other hand, cathode porosity exerts a more pronounced influence on discharge energy density than anode porosity at medium and high areal capacity. In the case of 10-minute fast charging, the impact of cathode porosity on cell energy density utilization is minimal across the three areal capacities, suggesting that cathode porosity does not a significant design constraint for fast charging. Nevertheless, anode porosity exerts a considerable influence on the cell's fast charging performance, as enhancing anode porosity can notably diminish cell polarization and augment energy utilization.

The relationship between thermodynamic and kinetic parameters, such as diffusion coefficient, conductivity, reaction rate constant of cell materials, and temperature is following the principles of Arrhenius' law. The changes in these parameters exhibit an exponential growth relationship with temperature, thereby demonstrating that the cell's fast charging and discharging performance is significantly affected by temperature. For many years, researchers have considered room temperature is the optimal operating temperature for lithium-ion batteries, however, our previous research indicated that as the energy density and the charge rate increase, the optimal operating temperature of the cell shifts to the high temperature range [31]. In this paper, the effect of temperature on the 3 energy densities is investigated. According to Fig. 3 , on the one hand, elevating the temperature can enhance the kinetic and thermodynamic processes, diminish polarization, and effectively improve the fast charging and discharging performance of the cell; on the other hand, elevating the temperature can expand the design space of the cell, allow the design parameters to fluctuate in a certain range, reduce the design and manufacturing requirements, and lower the production cost.

The aforementioned results demonstrate that battery performance is influenced by a multitude of design parameters and temperatures. Conventional battery design is based on empirical experiments, with a prolonged research and development cycle and considerable financial investment. Therefore, this paper proposes a design-oriented multi-objective optimization framework that melds high fidelity with computational efficiency, thereby facilitating rapid multi-objective optimization of cell design parameters.

In order to obtain accurate cell energy density, high-rate charging and discharging performance, most of the studies use P2D model for calculation. The P2D model employs a system of partial differential algebraic equations to determine the concentration and potential of the solid and liquid phases within the cell. This approach allows for the simulation of the cell's internal state from a physicochemical perspective, with high accuracy. Nevertheless, the P2D model is computationally expensive, requiring a considerable amount of time to reach a solution. On the contrary, the computational time required for machine learning models is significantly less than P2D model. However, the outputs of machine learning models are highly influenced by the training data, and they exhibit limited capacity to extrapolate predictions for data outside the range of the training data. To this end, the optimization framework proposed in this study combines the P2D model with a machine learning model, the P2D model generates cell performance data corresponding to multiple combinations of design parameters at different temperatures, which is used for the training of the machine learning surrogate model, thus realizing a fast and accurate mapping between cell design parameters and cell performance at different temperatures.

The efficient multi-objective optimization framework proposed in this paper is presented in Fig. 4 , which consists of 4 parts: the generation of parameter design space, the generation of training data by P2D model, the training of surrogate model, and multi-objective optimization. Firstly, within the design domain of the design parameters, the design of experiments is conducted through the Latin hypercube sampling method in order to obtain the parameter space, and the number of samples is minimized while ensuring the distribution is uniform and the design parameters are covered comprehensively. Subsequently, the cell performance data corresponding to multiple design parameter combinations at different temperatures are obtained through the simulation of the calibrated P2D model and employed as the training set for the surrogate model. Further, a machine learning model is employed to establish a mapping of cell design parameters and operating temperature to performance to obtain a high-fidelity, computationally efficient surrogate model. Finally, the surrogate model is combined with NSGA-|| algorithm to achieve fast optimization of battery performance.

With regard to the simulation platform, the P2D model is constructed and solved using the commercial software package COMSOL Multiphysics. Subsequently, a substantial training dataset is generated using COMSOL in conjunction with the LiveLink interface for MATLAB. The machine learning surrogate model is constructed and trained using MATLAB. Additionally, the optimization algorithm is also implemented using MATLAB.

In this study, a Feed-forward neural network (FFNN) is selected as the surrogate model to obtain complex nonlinear processing capabilities through composite mapping of simple nonlinear processing units. The surrogate model is a FFNN, which consists of one input layer, two hidden layers, and one output layer. Specifically, the input layer contains five neurons representing the operating temperature, cathode areal capacity, NP ratio, cathode porosity, and anode porosity. The hidden layers contain five neurons, and the output layer contains three neurons representing the 0.1C discharge energy density, 10-minute fast charging energy density, and 3C discharge energy density. The datasets are divided into training, validation, and test sets in the ratio of 6:2:2, and the FFNN is trained using the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm. The Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) are employed as performance evaluation indices of the surrogate model, and the calculation formulas are shown in Eqs. (1) and (2), where yi represents the real battery energy density, and y ̂i denotes the predicted battery energy density.
(1)
R M S E =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n

n

⌢
y
i
|
y i
× 100 %

The predicted results of the surrogate model are illustrated in Fig. 5 . The three energy densities predict results exhibit high accuracy, with the predicted RMSE of 3C discharge energy density representing the largest value among the three, but this value is only 1.43 Wh/kg, with the MAPE reaching 0.69 %. The maximum prediction error is within the acceptable range, thereby providing a model basis for high-precision and rapid iterative optimization.

Based on the above optimization framework, Section 4 firstly compares and analyzes the effects of different optimization objective calculation methods on the optimization results. Further, the cell design parameters at varying temperatures are optimized using a most balanced optimization approach to obtain optimal results. Finally, the cell operating temperature is introduced as a design variable, and the aging law of the vehicle under the New European Driving Cycle (NEDC) is considered to calculate the total driving range of the vehicle when the cell capacity is decayed to 80 %, and the total driving range is used as an optimization target. Subsequently, a multi-objective optimization is conducted to identify the optimal cell design parameters that achieve a balance between 0.1C discharge energy density, 10-min lithium plating-free fast charging energy density, 3C discharge energy density, and battery life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of three different objective calculation methods on optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization objective determines both the direction and the final result of optimization and plays a crucial role in optimization problems. This paper studies the impact of three different objective calculation methods on the three optimal energy density values. Case-1 directly uses 0.1C discharge energy density, 3C discharge energy density, and 10-min fast charging energy density as three objectives for multi-objective optimization, with equal weights for all three, and the final optimization objective will be converted to maximize the sum of the three energy densities. Case-2 optimization objectives are 0.1C discharge energy density, 3C discharge energy density/0.1C discharge energy density, 10 min fast charging energy density/0.1C discharge energy density, the ratio of the latter two indicates the cell energy density utilization rate of fast charging and discharging, and the larger the value, the smaller the battery polarization. Case-3 optimization objectives are the ratio of each energy density to its maximum value obtained from single-objective optimization, indicating the degree of proximity to the respective theoretical values, and the optimization result indicates the most balanced performance of the three energy densities.

The optimized results of the three different objective calculation methods are illustrated in Fig. 6a . The results of the optimization in three cases reveal that even at Case-1, the cell can be charged to 79.1 %SOC with 193 Wh/kg energy density within 10 min, proving the sufficient energy charged. Fig. 6b illustrates that the sum of the three energy densities of Case-1 is the largest, and it is easier to improve the 0.1C discharge energy density compared to improving the fast charging and discharging energy density, and by increasing the cathode areal capacity, the 0.1C discharge energy density has been significantly improved in spite of sacrificing a small part of the fast charging and fast discharging performance. Fig. 6c illustrates that Case-2 has the lowest electrolyte concentration gradient along the thickness direction at the end of the charging and discharging processes, proving that the optimized results of Case2 have the optimal cell utilization and the minimum polarization. In addition, Case-2 has the highest 10-min fast charging energy density, primarily due to the fast-charging energy density is easier to improve than 3C discharge energy density, and the iterative optimization process is inherently biased towards the more readily attainable outcomes. Fig. 6d illustrates that Case-3 has the smallest distance of the three energy densities from the mean value, indicating that its optimization results can achieve the greatest balance between the 0.1C discharge energy density, the 3C discharge energy density, and the 10-min fast charging energy density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature on multi-objective optimization
ce:section.ce:para: As indicated in Section 2.2, increasing the cell operating temperature can enhance the high-rate charging and discharging performances. Accordingly, this section optimizes the cell design parameters at three temperatures (25 °C, 40 °C, and 60 °C) through the most balanced optimal objective calculation method.

Fig.7a illustrates that the multi-objective optimal performance at 60 °C can completely cover the single-objective optimal performance at 25 °C, indicating that increasing the temperature can significantly improve the 0.1C discharge energy density, 3C discharge energy density, and 10 min charging energy density simultaneously, and alleviate the contradiction between high energy density and fast charging and discharging. Fig.7b illustrates the results of the optimal design parameters at varying temperatures. It can be observed that the cathode areal capacity can be enhanced by ∼1.45 times relative to 25 °C by increasing the temperature to 60 °C. The NP ratio also increases, and the cathode and anode porosity decrease due to the elevated temperature mitigating the cell polarization. Consequently, elevating cell operating temperature reduces the necessity for rigorous cell design, permits the utilization of high cathode areal capacity with low electrode porosity, and expands the scope for cell design while achieving a balance between energy density and high-rate charging and discharging performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature as an additional optimization variable by considering cell degradation
ce:section.ce:para: While increasing temperature can enhance the cell energy density, fast charging and discharging performance, prolonged exposure to high temperatures can result in accelerated growth of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) and capacity loss. To achieve a balance between the 0.1C discharge energy density, 3C discharge energy density, 10-min charging energy density, and life, temperature is introduced as a new design variable. Optimization of cell design parameters and temperature is carried out considering cell aging. Thus, this paper takes the optimization of the design parameters of a single cell of a 40 kWh EV battery pack as an example [32]. The 10-min maximum lithium plating-free multi-stage constant current method proposed in this paper is used as the charging protocol and the NEDC serves as the discharge benchmark. The optimization process considers calendar aging and cycling aging under real vehicle conditions. Optimization objectives are set to achieve the 0.1C discharging energy density, 3C discharging energy density, 10-min charging energy density, and the total driving range of the vehicle under balanced conditions. For calendar aging, the temperature is fixed at 25 °C. The calendar and cycling aging are calculated with reference [33], and their degradation curves are presented in Fig. S6. For the sake of simplicity, the single-day driving time is fixed to be 1.66 h [34].

The impact of temperature and cell design parameters on the total driving range of the vehicle is illustrated in Fig. 8 . It should be noted that the effect of temperature on the total driving range of the vehicle is not monotonous. At low temperatures, the cell exhibits reduced charging and discharging performance, despite the cell aging more slowly at these temperatures. At elevated temperatures, the cell is capable of both greater charging and discharging, as well as an increased single-cycle driving range. However, the cell is susceptible to severe decline at high temperatures, leading to a reduction in the number of cycles. As temperature varies from low to high, the driving range per cycle increases, though in decreasing increments. The total number of cycles decreases with the increasing aging rate. Consequently, the total range of the vehicle first increases and then decreases with temperature reaching an optimum value at the optimal temperature. In addition, the cell design parameters significantly influence the vehicle's driving range. Fast charging and discharging performances at a high areal capacity decline significantly, resulting in a reduction of the single-cycle driving range and a subsequent reduction in the vehicle's overall driving range (Fig. 8a). The effects of NP ratio and positive electrode porosity on the vehicle's overall driving range are illustrated in Fig. 8b-c. As for NP ratio and positive electrode porosity, a minimal influence is observed for fast charging performance and overall driving range. Conversely, the effects of negative electrode porosity on the vehicle's overall driving range are depicted in Fig. 8d. As negative electrode porosity increases, the cell's fast charging performance improves, and both the single-cycle driving range and the total driving range expand.

In this paper, we investigate the single-objective optimization of maximizing total mileage and multi-objective optimization with a balance of 0.1C discharge energy density, 3C discharge energy density, 10-min charging energy density, and the total mileage, both assuming a 10-minute fast-charging energy density of the cell exceeding 180 Wh/kg. To facilitate comparison with the performance of the cells after temperature optimization, this study conducts optimization at two fixed temperatures, 25 °C and 60 °C. Fig. 9a –b illustrates the optimized cell design parameters, temperature, fast-charging and fast-discharging performances, and total driving range in single-objective optimization. At 25 °C, battery life will decrease significantly if the charging C-rate is increased. Furthermore, the total driving range is highly sensitive to the charging C-rates. To satisfy the goal of 180 Wh/kg in10-min fast charging, low areal capacity and high anode and cathode porosity are selected as optimization results. At 60 °C, though a gradual decrease in cycle number with an increase of charging C-rates, the cell is severely degraded at high temperatures, resulting in a higher sensitivity to temperatures. Thus, the optimization of cell parameters entails an increase in the charging C-rate to enhance the performance of fast charging and discharging. In this case, the anode porosity is 0.38, approaching the upper limit of the design parameter. Compared with the results after optimization at 25 °C and 60 °C, the cell in the optimal case has moderate areal capacity, and lower negative electrode porosity, and the optimal temperature is 37 °C, which can warm up to improve the fast charging and discharging ability, and avoid the rapid aging caused by higher temperature, and finally achieve a total mileage of 148,000 km.

To fully equalize the cell performance and optimize both the single-cycle performance and the total vehicle range, multi-objectives are optimized and the results are shown in Fig. 9c-d. The 25 °C optimized cell design parameters have higher areal capacity and porosity relative to the single-objective optimization, resulting in greater 0.1C discharge energy density and fast-charging and fast-discharging performance, but with a partial loss of driving range due to the higher charging C-rate. Due to the reduced demand of maximum total driving range in multi-objective optimization, the optimization at 60 °C is primarily intended to enhance the 0.1C energy density and fast charging and discharging performances. When the optimization is conducted for an unrestricted temperature, the optimization outcomes demonstrate that both the areal capacity and porosity are enhanced in comparison to that of the single-objective optimization. Additionally, the temperature is elevated to 42 °C. Although a decrease in overall mileage by approximately 10,000 km is observed, notable enhancements are achieved both in the 0.1C discharging energy density and the 10-min fast charging energy density.

This paper presents a systematic study of the effects of four cell design parameters-cathode areal capacity, NP ratio, cathode and anode porosity, and temperature on the 0.1C discharge energy density, 10-min lithium plating free fast charging energy density, 3C discharge energy density, and battery life. The combination of a physics-based P2D model and the feed-forward neural network surrogate model with the NSGA-II optimization algorithm allows for the establishment of a high-fidelity and high-computational-efficiency multi-objective optimization framework. The effects of three different objective calculating methods are compared to find three energy densities sum maximum, lowest polarization, and most balanced respectively. The cell design parameters are optimized at different temperatures in the most balanced optimization method, and the results demonstrate that increasing operating temperature not only comprehensively improves cell energy density, but also alleviates constraints and broadens design parameters. Furthermore, operating temperature is added as a design variable and cell aging under NEDC is considered in order to achieve the balance of energy density, fast charging and discharging density, and life.

Due to the limited resources in cell fabrication, the P2D model in this work is calibrated against only three cell designs. In practical battery designs in the future, if resources were available, we would suggest generating a more comprehensive dataset consisting of cells with a much larger matrix of design parameters for P2D model calibration. This dataset could also serve as a benchmark for training the surrogate model, potentially further improving the accuracy of the proposed models. Additionally, the weights of each objective in the presented optimization framework can be flexibly tailored in practical battery design to meet specific battery requirements for a given application.

Xiao-Ying Ma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Wen-Ke Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Ying Yin: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology. Kailong Liu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Xiao-Guang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.egyai.2024.100416.
